dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a priority 1 chest pain at --Address Redacted-- in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've got chest pain and some shortness of breath. It started about 30 minutes ago.
Let's test your responsiveness. Are you alert to time, place, and person?
It's Thursday, around 7:30 PM, I'm on my porch at home with no one else around.
You're alert and oriented to time, place, and person - that's good to hear. GCS is 15.
Your airway is patent and clear. You can speak comfortably - that's reassuring.
Your breathing does not appear labored or in distress at this time.
Let me check your breathing rate. You're breathing at 40 times per minute - that's a bit high, we'll monitor it.
Your pulse is strong and regular. I don't see any signs of active bleeding.
Your skin is warm, dry, and intact with no signs of jaundice, rashes, or swelling.
Your pupils are equal, round, and reactive at about 4 mm - no signs of tracheal deviation or facial droop.
No nasal discharge, obstruction, or redness noted in the throat - no swollen tonsils.
Chest rise is even and breath sounds are clear and equal bilaterally.
Your abdomen is flat, soft, and not tender on palpation with normal bowel sounds present.
The pelvic area is stable with no signs of instability or deformity.
No deformities or angulation noted. Skin integrity appears intact and intact on trunk and extremities.
Peripheral pulses are present and equal in both arms and legs.
Your respiratory rate is 40 breaths per minute - higher than normal, but we've seen other signs consistent with distress.
Our pulse oximeter shows 80% - we'll address that now.
Pulse is 95 beats per minute and is regular in rhythm.
Your blood pressure is 177 over 104 - we'll continue monitoring this closely.
Glucose reading shows low or equipment failure to detect. This is consistent with known renal disease from EPCR.
Any other symptoms have you been experiencing?
No, just chest pain and some shortness of breath.
What were you doing before the symptoms began?
I was just sitting on my porch. I didn't notice anything that triggered it.
When did you last eat or drink anything?
I had some soup and juice about two hours ago.
Can you tell me what medications you take regularly?
Hydralazine, amlodipine, Clonidine, Isosorbide, Baclofen - you know the list is long.
Any medication or food allergies?
No known allergies.
When did the chest pain start for you?
Roughly 30 minutes ago - sudden and stayed there.
Were you doing anything when the chest pain started?
Sitting on my porch doing nothing.
How would you describe the pain - pressure, sharp, or something else?
Squeezing and tightening - more like pressure than sharp pain.
Does the pain spread anywhere else?
No - remains in my chest only.
I need to know how severe it feels using your own description.
It's noticeable but not so bad I'd rate it a 4.
What other medical conditions do you have?
I have end stage kidney disease, old heart attack, stroke history, COPD, depression, anxiety - and more.
I'm going to give you 324 mg of aspirin as per our protocol to help prevent clotting - is there a reason not to proceed?
I can take that - no issues.
I'm placing you on a 4-lead cardiac monitor - please sit still for a moment.
EKG tracing shows sinus rhythm with some arrhythmia - nothing alarming at this point.
I'll now take a 12-lead ECG to get more detailed view of cardiac activity for better interpretation.
Given your symptoms and medical history, we'll transport you to Chippenham Hospital for appropriate evaluation.
I agree with the destination. The emergency department is aware of your estimated time of arrival.
I'll help move you to the stretcher now and secure it for the transport to the hospital.
I'm providing non-rebreather at 15 liters per minute to help boost your oxygen level.
You are safely secured on the stretcher and ready for transport.
We've informed the emergency department at Chippenham and provided details about your condition and current interventions.
Your updated vitals are 86 pulse, 32 respiratory rate, with improved SpO₂ to 90% and blood pressure increased to 190/114.
Oxygen remains effective and will be continued during transport and as needed.
The EKG repeat shows sinus rhythm - no new or worsening issues found.
The hospital team has been made aware of your recent vitals and that you've refused IV access with aspirin administered.
Your oxygen supply will remain attached during transport and will be assessed upon arrival.
No drug allergies or contradictions were found and documented in the EPCR for the administered medications.
You are ready for transport. I have secured the stretcher and gear.
One more cycle of vitals shows 86 pulse, 32 respirations, 90% oxygen saturation, and 190/114 blood pressure.
Any change in your level of chest tightness or discomfort since departure?
It's come down a bit - I would say a 3 now.
The ride should be smooth as per the weather and traffic.
We have accounted for all staging per facility requirements for your arrival into Room 30.
Once on ground, the nursing team will take over immediately and ensure continuity of care.
The hospital ER is expecting you and has a plan in place for your full evaluation.
Once we arrive, nurses will wheel you directly to Room 30 and receive the full patient handover.
Any more questions or concerns before we start our journey?
That's all then, we'll depart with you in good condition.
Safe and sound to the next step - let the hospital team know what you might need.
